draft.introduction: The Seafarer (c. 10th century) is an Old English elegy from the Exeter Book in which a speaker describes the hardships of life at sea and transforms them into a meditation on earthly exile and the longing for an eternal homeland.

translation.text: Indeed the joys of the Lord are warmer to me than this dead, transitory life on land. I do not believe that earthly wealth stands forever. Always, in every case, one of three things will bring uncertainty before one’s final day: illness, or age, or sword-hate will wrest life from the doomed.
translation.original_text: Forþon mē hatran sind Dryhtnes drēamas þonne þis dēade līf, lǣne on londe. Ic gelȳfe nō þæt him eorðwelan ēce stondeð.

translator_notes.note: The tension between earthly sorrow and divine joy has generated centuries of debate: is the speaker a literal sailor or an allegorical pilgrim? The poem resists resolution. Its power lies in the unresolved fusion of Germanic stoicism and Christian eschatology.
